items.title: Altman and Amodei both walk back their AI 'jobs apocalypse' predictions — just before OpenAI and Anthropic IPO
items.summary: Within a 48-hour window, the CEOs of OpenAI and Anthropic publicly walked back the two most-cited 'AI will gut white-collar work' predictions of the last 18 months. Speaking at a Commonwealth Bank of Australia conference in Sydney on May 26, OpenAI's Sam Altman said 'I'm delighted to be wrong about this — I thought there would have been more impact on entry-level white-collar jobs being eliminated by now than has actually happened,' adding that his 2022-vintage technical predictions about ChatGPT had been 'roughly right' but his predictions on the social and economic impact had been 'pretty wrong.' Anthropic's Dario Amodei — who in mid-2025 forecast AI could eliminate 50% of entry-level white-collar jobs in five years — reframed the same shift earlier in May with 'if you automate 90% of the job, then everyone does the 10% of the job.' By May 27 the story had gone wire-wide (BNN Bloomberg, TIME, AI Magazine, Breitbart all ran it), and the framing sharpened around a second pressure the CEOs are now navigating: US public-opinion polling shows rising discontent over the AI-driven disruption that tech and political leaders spent two years predicting, so the earlier doom-laden statements have become a reputational liability — not just an investor-roadshow problem but a trust problem with the public these labs need as customers. The timing thread is unchanged: Anthropic is closing a $30B round at a ~$900B valuation (already covered in the May 25 pm) and OpenAI is preparing to file its confidential S-1 with the SEC, with the most-cited IPO window being Q4 2026 / early 2027. The Yale Budget Lab data Altman cited in Sydney is the empirical lever for both reversals — Yale found no statistically significant change in occupational mix or unemployment duration in high-AI-exposure jobs since ChatGPT shipped in late 2022. The counter-data point both CEOs are careful not to dwell on: tech-sector layoffs YTD have already crossed 115,000 — approaching the 124,000 logged in all of 2025 — with Meta (8,000-cut wave covered May 23), Amazon (~30,000-role reduction over five months), and Snap (1,000-cut, 16%-of-staff reduction) each citing AI as a stated driver. The reframe both CEOs are converging on — augmentation rather than substitution, output multiplication rather than headcount substitution — is the public framing two pre-IPO frontier labs need their roadshows to land on.
items.why_it_matters: This is the cleanest narrative-turn moment in the AI-and-labor story since GPT-4. For 18 months the framing has been 'frontier-lab CEOs warn AI will gut entry-level white-collar work,' and within a single week the two most-quoted CEOs in that frame walked it back — Altman explicitly ('I was pretty wrong'), Amodei more carefully ('automate 90% of the job → everyone does the 10%'). The thing to internalize is the asymmetric incentive structure that produced the reversal: an apocalyptic frame helps a frontier-lab CEO sound serious in 2024 but is a liability on an IPO roadshow in 2026, and both companies happen to be filing in the same window. The newer wrinkle, now that the story has gone wire-wide, is the public-opinion pressure: US polling shows real discontent over the disruption that was promised, so the apocalyptic framing isn't just a roadshow liability, it's a trust liability with the consumers and enterprises these labs are selling to. The Yale Budget Lab data is the substantive part — eighteen months in, the occupational-mix and unemployment-duration numbers in high-AI-exposure jobs really do show no measurable break, which means the 'AI is silently eating jobs' story is now competing with a real-world data set that doesn't show it. For a coder thinking about your own stack, two practical reads: first, the augmentation framing (you doing the 10% of the job, AI doing the 90%) is the one that actually matches what Claude Code or Cursor does in a Python project, and is much more useful to plan around than the apocalypse framing; second, watch the underlying signal — layoff headlines that cite AI haven't slowed (115K in five months, already approaching all of 2025's 124K), so the labor pressure is real even if it isn't showing up in the aggregate Yale numbers yet. The honest middle position is what both CEOs are now publicly converging on, and is also probably right.
items.why_it_matters_brief: Both frontier-lab CEOs walked back their 'AI will gut entry-level jobs' predictions the same week — as IPO roadshows loom and US polling shows public discontent over the disruption they promised.

revisions.change: added wire-wide pickup (BNN Bloomberg/TIME May 27) and the US public-opinion-discontent angle; tightened the layoff number (115K YTD approaching all-of-2025's 124K); updated why_it_matters and brief

takes.take: Centers the IPO-timing read: both Altman and Amodei are reframing in the same window their companies are preparing for trillion-dollar public-market entries, and an apocalyptic labor story doesn't sell at roadshow.
Lays out both quotes side-by-side: Altman 'pretty wrong' on entry-level cuts, Amodei's 'automate 90% / everyone does the 10%' substitution-to-augmentation reframing.
Quotes Altman at the CBA Sydney conference admitting 'my intuitions were just off' on the near-term social and economic impact of ChatGPT-class systems, even though his technical predictions held up.

Anchors the reversal in real data: Altman cited Yale Budget Lab findings that no statistically significant change in occupational mix or unemployment duration has been observed in high-AI-exposure jobs since late 2022.

The May 27 wire syndication that pushed the story mainstream — frames it as 'AI chiefs walk back job apocalypse warnings' and surfaces the public-backlash angle: the earlier doom-laden statements 'have now come to haunt the AI industry' as US polling shows discontent over predicted disruption.

The dissenting take: 115K+ tech layoffs YTD with Meta, Amazon, and Snap each citing AI is itself a structural shock even if the aggregate numbers don't show an apocalypse — the reversal is real but partial.

items.title: Heretic strips safety guardrails from Meta Llama 3.3 and Google Gemma 3 in 10 minutes — Meta and Google now respond, calling abliteration a 'known challenge'
items.summary: A joint Financial Times and AI-safety-group Alice investigation (published May 25-26) demonstrated that a free GitHub-hosted tool called Heretic, created by Philipp Emanuel Weidmann, can strip the safety alignment from Meta's open-weight Llama 3.3 and Google's open-weight Gemma 3 in roughly 10 minutes on consumer hardware, using ~4 lines of code and no specialist GPUs. The technique — 'abliteration' — is architecturally distinct from prompt-based jailbreaking: it directly modifies the model weights to remove the refusal direction in residual-stream activations, so the model no longer experiences refusal as an option rather than being talked around it. Once stripped, both models complied with prompts the original versions refused — Gemma 3 generated working malware to steal credit-card data and gave step-by-step instructions for dispersing lethal chlorine gas in a crowded room; Llama 3.3 calculated a specific per-kilogram lethal dose of ricin. The scale numbers are the durable framing: Heretic's creator confirmed to FT that the tool has been used to produce more than 3,500 'decensored' model variants, which have together accumulated over 13 million downloads on public repositories. The new development overnight is that both vendors have now responded to the FT for the first time. Meta declined to comment on the record, but a person close to the company pointed to its Advanced AI Scaling Framework — Meta says it assesses open models' capabilities before release and withholds any version judged a 'catastrophic' risk unless it finds sufficient mitigations. Google acknowledged the risk directly, calling abliteration 'a known technical challenge facing all open models' and saying its open models 'undergo rigorous internal safety evaluations prior to launch to help prevent these kinds of troubling examples.' Google's 'known challenge' phrasing is effectively a concession that the technique isn't currently solvable at the weights level. Coverage (FT, Irish Times, eWeek, Futurism, Techstrong.ai, News9live, Buddy Learner, CryptoDnes) places this directly against the EU AI Act timeline: under the May 7 Digital Omnibus agreement, generative-AI watermarking obligations kick in December 2, 2026 — but watermarks are output-side and don't survive an abliteration pass that mutates the model itself. This is also the same week as the May 25 Vatican encyclical urging slower AI development, and the contrast is the substantive one: as Pope Leo XIV's 'Magnifica Humanitas' was calling for stronger guardrails, free software was demonstrating that the guardrails on the most-downloaded open-weight families come off in 10 minutes — and the model vendors are now publicly conceding it.
items.why_it_matters: The open-weight-safety question has been theoretical for two years — 'in principle alignment trained into a downloadable model can be removed.' The Heretic + abliteration result is the concrete demonstration that turns the theoretical objection into a deployed, measured one: 4 lines of code, 10 minutes on a laptop, 3,500 stripped variants, 13M downloads. The technical detail to absorb is why abliteration is harder to fix than jailbreaking: a jailbreak prompt routes around the refusal direction in the model's activations, but abliteration overwrites that direction in the weights, so the stripped model isn't a model-being-tricked, it's a model that no longer has the refusal concept at all. The newest signal is that Meta and Google have now both responded — and neither claims a fix. Meta points to a pre-release capability assessment (a decision about what to release, not a property of the released weights), and Google explicitly calls abliteration 'a known technical challenge facing all open models,' which is as close to a public concession as a frontier lab gives that the problem is currently unsolved at the layer where it matters. That's a fundamentally different problem for any safety strategy that assumes the model itself is part of the defense, and it's also the problem the EU AI Act's December 2 watermarking rules and the broader output-side filtering approaches can't solve, because both operate on the deployed model rather than on the weights. For a coder, the practical read: any project that lets users bring their own open-weight model needs to assume the model behind the API has had abliteration run on it, because the cost-and-time gap between 'aligned model' and 'stripped model' is now essentially zero. The deeper read is that this is the cleanest evidence yet that 'open weights' and 'safety baked into the model' may not be a coherent combination going forward, which is going to land in every open-weight licensing and policy debate from here.
items.why_it_matters_brief: Abliteration overwrites the refusal direction in the weights in ~10 minutes — and Google now calls it 'a known technical challenge facing all open models,' a public concession it isn't solvable at the weights layer.

revisions.change: added Meta and Google's first on-record responses to the FT (Meta's Advanced AI Scaling Framework, Google calling abliteration 'a known technical challenge facing all open models'); updated title, summary, why_it_matters, and brief to center the vendors' concession

takes.take: Leads on the time-to-strip numbers — Heretic removes guardrails from Llama 3.3 in under 10 minutes, no specialist hardware required, four lines of code — and notes the experiments produced chlorine-gas dispersion instructions and credit-card-stealing malware.

Carries the vendor responses: Meta declined to comment but pointed to its Advanced AI Scaling Framework (assesses capabilities pre-release, withholds 'catastrophic'-risk versions unless mitigated), while Google called abliteration 'a known technical challenge facing all open models' and cited 'rigorous internal safety evaluations prior to launch.'

Centers the prompt examples that worked after stripping: chlorine-gas attacks, malware generation, child-sexual-abuse content — and frames the open-weight safety question as having moved from theoretical to demonstrated.
Quotes Heretic creator Philipp Emanuel Weidmann confirming the 3,500 decensored variants and 13M cumulative download figure, which is the durable scale framing of the story.

Explains the architectural distinction between abliteration (modifies the model's refusal direction in weights) and prompt jailbreaking (routes around the same direction), which is why standard safety training doesn't survive the procedure.

Surfaces the broader policy implication: the EU AI Act's December 2026 watermarking obligations are output-side and don't reach a process that mutates the weights themselves, so the regulatory toolkit has a gap.
Frames it as the most concrete evidence to date that 'open weights' and 'baked-in safety alignment' may not be a coherent combination in the long run, regardless of training methodology.

items.title: Microsoft expands Copilot Studio computer-using agents to every commercial geo — adds voice agents, GA agent-to-agent, OpenAI CUA + Claude Sonnet 4.5
items.summary: Microsoft's May 26 Copilot Studio update broadens the May 13 GA of computer-using agents from a regional rollout to all commercial Power Platform geographies — making Copilot Studio the first major hyperscaler shipping production-grade vision-and-reasoning computer use to enterprises everywhere it sells Power Platform. The May 26 release ships several layered changes on top of the original GA. First, the geographic expansion plus a new orchestration layer Microsoft says improves evaluation accuracy ~20% while cutting net token consumption ~50% versus the May 13 build. Second, the GA model set is now explicitly OpenAI CUA and Anthropic Claude Sonnet 4.5, with Azure Key Vault for credential storage, Microsoft Purview audit logging, and a configurable human-in-the-loop review step — i.e., a production governance stack rather than a research preview. Third, alongside computer use, Microsoft GA'd a redesigned workflow builder and real-time voice agents. Overnight coverage (ADTmag, Windows Forum) added detail the launch-day writeups underplayed: agent-to-agent (A2A) communication is now generally available in Copilot Studio — agents can exchange information, delegate tasks, and coordinate with one another inside the same governed environment — the real-time voice agents are GA specifically in North America through Dynamics 365 Contact Center rather than worldwide, and Work IQ extensibility shipped in the same push. The framing across coverage (Microsoft Community Hub, Windows News, DevOps.com, ADTmag, Enterprise DNA, Microsoft Copilot Studio blog) is that this is the first time the agents-controlling-arbitrary-software story is generally available in an enterprise platform with credential vaulting, audit logging, HITL review, and now GA multi-agent delegation in the same release, rather than a research preview from one of the frontier labs. The competitive sequence — Anthropic Computer Use (originally Oct 2024), OpenAI Codex Locked Use on macOS (May 21, covered in 5/25-pm), Microsoft Copilot Studio CUA GA expansion (May 13/26) — locates Microsoft as the first to land the integrated enterprise-governance package rather than the technical capability.
items.why_it_matters: The interesting bit isn't the computer-use capability — Anthropic shipped that in October 2024 and OpenAI shipped the Mac-locked version last week. The interesting bit is what Microsoft has now bolted to it: Azure Key Vault for credentials, Purview audit logging, configurable human-in-the-loop review, OpenAI CUA and Claude Sonnet 4.5 as plug-in GA models, GA agent-to-agent delegation, and a ~50% token-cost reduction versus the May 13 build. That's the first time the agents-driving-real-apps story has shown up as an enterprise package with the governance plumbing in the same release rather than as a research demo, and it's the inflection point that matters for whether large organizations will actually deploy this stuff in 2026. The A2A piece going GA is the under-noticed part: multi-agent delegation (one agent handing a subtask to another) is now inside the same governed boundary as the credential vault and audit log, which is the missing piece for the 'team of agents' pattern everyone has been demoing. The architectural read that ties this to the AlphaProof Nexus result covered earlier this week: every reliable agent product in 2026 is the same shape — a frontier LLM (Sonnet 4.5 or OpenAI CUA here, Gemini 3.1 Pro in AlphaProof) does the proposal, and a deterministic verifier (Lean for math, Purview/HITL/Key Vault for enterprise) decides what leaves the loop. The proposing model is the headline but the verifier is what's actually shipping the product. For a Python or HTML/CSS coder, the practical read: if a small business or a side project starts asking you to wire up automation across multiple SaaS apps, the path of least resistance has shifted in the last 30 days from 'glue scripts and Selenium' to 'Copilot Studio CUA + a few human checkpoints,' and the labor-and-cost math has changed accordingly. Watch for the same shape from Google Vertex AI Agent Builder and AWS Bedrock Agents in the next two quarters — once the verifier-side governance is the differentiator, that's where the competition will move.
items.why_it_matters_brief: First time computer-use agents ship with Key Vault credentials, Purview audit logging, HITL review and now GA agent-to-agent delegation in one governed release — the enterprise plumbing is what's actually new.

revisions.change: added that agent-to-agent (A2A) communication is now GA, that real-time voice agents are GA specifically in North America via Dynamics 365 Contact Center, and Work IQ extensibility; fixed cross-reference to the AlphaProof Nexus item (now covered earlier this week, not this morning); updated title, summary, why_it_matters, and brief

takes.take: Microsoft's own framing: computer use, voice agents, and the redesigned workflow builder all GA in the same release, with the new orchestration layer reported at ~20% better eval accuracy and ~50% lower token consumption than the May 13 build.

Adds the detail launch-day pieces missed: agent-to-agent (A2A) communication is now GA in Copilot Studio (agents delegate and coordinate inside one governed environment), and the real-time voice agents are GA in North America through Dynamics 365 Contact Center rather than every geography.

Centers the 'first major hyperscaler to reach production-grade computer use' framing and treats it as the moment vision-and-reasoning automation crosses the production-readiness threshold rather than a frontier-lab demo.

Walks through the governance stack — Azure Key Vault, Purview audit logging, configurable HITL — and argues that's the load-bearing differentiator vs Anthropic/OpenAI computer use, not the underlying CUA capability itself.

Frames it as 'AI agents that can use any software' and notes the GA model set explicitly includes both OpenAI's CUA and Anthropic's Claude Sonnet 4.5 — a multi-vendor agent stack from Microsoft, not a single-model bet.

items.title: Beijing extends travel restrictions to private AI labs — Alibaba and DeepSeek researchers now need government pre-approval to go abroad
items.summary: China's government agencies began requiring private-sector AI founders, researchers, and executives at firms including Alibaba and DeepSeek to obtain government approval before traveling overseas, Bloomberg reported May 26. The expansion marks a threshold shift: Beijing has long restricted the international mobility of state-affiliated researchers, nuclear scientists, and state-enterprise executives, but extending mandatory pre-approval to the private AI sector is new. Individuals are added to a restriction list based on the strategic importance of their specific work rather than job title or seniority — meaning a mid-level researcher on a project Beijing deems critical can be restricted while a more senior executive on less sensitive work is not. The policy had been quietly applied to some DeepSeek executives since December 2025; the May 26 Bloomberg report confirms it as a formal, broader private-sector policy rather than informal guidance. Prior to May 2025, Beijing merely 'advised' prominent AI figures to avoid US travel; this move converts soft guidance into hard pre-approval. The most concrete enforcement case to date is Manus: two co-founders of the Singapore-based AI startup were separately barred from overseas travel while Chinese regulators investigated Meta's $2.8 billion acquisition of the firm — a high-profile enforcement episode that predated and likely helped set the template for the broader private-sector expansion. The story received significant Asia-Pacific overnight pickup: Japan Times (May 27), The Edge Singapore (May 27), Australia's Information Age/ACS (May 27-28), and Fortune (May 27).
items.why_it_matters: The framing that makes this a threshold story rather than background geopolitics is the private-sector extension. For three years, the working assumption in US-China AI analysis has been that Chinese private labs — DeepSeek, Alibaba/Qwen, Moonshot/Kimi, MiniMax — operate differently from state actors: they have market incentives, international research connections, and talent mobility that state entities lack. Beijing has now partially collapsed that distinction. When pre-approval travel controls cover startup founders and researchers at private firms based on the strategic importance of their specific work, the regulatory relationship between those firms and the state has structurally shifted even if the ownership structure has not. Three concrete threads to track: first, international conference attendance and cross-border collaboration now route through an approval layer for the individuals Beijing considers most valuable — this shapes what Chinese research gets presented abroad and who can build cross-border professional networks; second, researchers who might consider relocating to international companies face an explicit departure-approval process that changes the mobility calculus for the most mobile segment of Chinese AI talent; third, the Chinese labs with the strongest open-weight releases (DeepSeek and Alibaba/Qwen are exactly the firms whose researchers are now subject to pre-approval) produced the open-weight sprint that has kept pace with US frontier labs for two years — the coordination and publication pipeline that made that sprint fast now has a new friction point for the individuals driving it. The Manus enforcement case is the sharpest end-state signal: two co-founders who had already relocated to Singapore were still barred from further travel while regulators reviewed a US acquisition, which means the restrictions follow the individual rather than stopping at China's border. For a coder watching the open-weight ecosystem, model weights still cross borders without their researchers, so near-term open-weight release cadence is probably less affected than the research-community and talent-exchange layer — but watch for longer-term effects if the most mobile researchers respond by moving before the controls tighten further.
items.why_it_matters_brief: Beijing extended state-style travel controls to private AI labs — DeepSeek and Alibaba researchers now need government pre-approval to travel abroad, the first time China's private AI workforce has been treated as a national-security asset.

revisions.change: created; gap item — Bloomberg May 26 original, Japan Times / The Edge Singapore / Fortune / ACS overnight Asia-Pacific pickup May 27-28; flagged for this window by the 2am enrichment run

takes.take: The original report: China expanding travel curbs to top AI talent at private firms, requiring government approval before overseas travel for founders, researchers, and executives at Alibaba and DeepSeek who are considered strategically important regardless of seniority.

Asia overnight pickup; frames the policy as part of a broader pattern of Beijing tightening strategic controls on technology, noting the December 2025 quiet application to DeepSeek executives as the opening move before the private-sector expansion.

Leads with the Manus enforcement case as the most concrete example: co-founders already in Singapore were barred from further travel while regulators reviewed Meta's $2.8B acquisition — the clearest signal that restrictions follow the individual rather than stopping at China's border.

Singapore-angle framing: given that Manus co-founders relocated to Singapore and were still restricted, the policy has direct implications for Chinese AI talent across regional hubs throughout Southeast Asia.

Frames it as 'China restricts travel of AI experts amid tech war' — Australia/Pacific-region tech community pickup; connects to the structural competition for AI talent between Western countries and China.

items.title: Daniel Stenberg's 'The Pressure' — curl is now drowning in actually-good AI-assisted security reports, but they're all LOW/MEDIUM severity
items.summary: Lead curl maintainer Daniel Stenberg published a follow-up post on May 26 titled 'The pressure' that updates his earlier (January 2026) decision to wind down curl's HackerOne bug-bounty program. The new wrinkle: where the January story was 'curl drowning in low-effort AI slop reports,' the May 26 story is the inverse — the rate of incoming security reports is now 4-5x higher than 2024 and ~2x higher than 2025, but the quality is, in his words, 'way higher than ever before.' The curl team is now averaging more than one detailed, long, plausible security report per day. With about half of the current release cycle left, curl already has twelve confirmed vulnerabilities — a new project record — and the project will hit 30 published CVEs in 2026 before the calendar reaches half-year, by far the highest count in curl's history. A clarifying detail the digest framing should carry: Stenberg is explicit that this flood is not producing catastrophic bugs — 'almost no one finds terrible vulnerabilities,' and every curl vulnerability found in the last few years has been rated LOW or MEDIUM severity. So the pressure is a triage-volume problem (one good report per day, every one needing careful human assessment), not a severity spike. Stenberg's forward warning is the durable line: 'this avalanche is going to make maintainer overload even worse,' and projects without added maintainers will struggle to absorb the backlog. He also notes this is the first time in 25 years that his wife has voiced concern about his work-life balance. The pairing with the Mythos/Glasswing disclosure-pipeline story covered earlier this week is the key framing: Anthropic's Mythos and ZeroPath-class tools are the upstream of the same firehose — a closed-weight frontier model autonomously finds and reproduces a bug, the disclosure goes to a maintainer like Stenberg, and the patch-and-verify becomes the maintainer's full-time job. (Stenberg has been pointed about the hype around AI bug-hunting — he called Anthropic's Mythos 'the greatest marketing stunt ever' on May 11.) Simon Willison linked the post the same day; LWN, The New Stack, and noise.getoto.net mirrored it.
items.why_it_matters: If you only read the Mythos story earlier this week, you get half the picture: Anthropic's closed-weight model finds bugs end-to-end, the disclosure pipeline becomes the bottleneck. Stenberg's post is the other half — what the bottleneck actually feels like from inside curl, the most-used networking library on Earth. The substantive update from January is that the report quality is now genuinely high; this isn't the 'AI slop' problem curl ended its bounty over in January, it's the opposite problem: real bugs, found by capable systems, arriving faster than two volunteer-maintainer humans can responsibly triage and ship fixes for. But the nuance that sharpens (and partly corrects) the Mythos pairing is severity: Stenberg is explicit that almost none of these are terrible bugs — every curl vuln found in recent years has been rated LOW or MEDIUM. So the failure mode here is not a catastrophic-vulnerability spike slipping through; it's maintainer burnout from triage volume. The 4-5x-over-2024 rate, the 12-vulns-with-half-cycle-left record, and the 30-CVEs-before-half-year trajectory are the load-bearing numbers, and Stenberg's note that his wife has — for the first time in 25 years — voiced concern about his hours is the human read on a structural shift. For a Python or HTML/CSS coder, the practical lesson isn't about curl specifically; it's about the maintenance-cost shape of every dependency you pull in. The same firehose hits OpenSSL, wolfSSL, zlib, libcurl, the Python standard library, every npm package, every PyPI package. The disclosure-and-patch pipeline that has been roughly stable since the late-1990s is now operating against an input rate it wasn't designed for, and the failure mode is not a vulnerability slipping through, it's the maintainer burning out and walking away. Plan your dependency choices like a curl maintainer might leave tomorrow, because in a different open-source project they already have.
items.why_it_matters_brief: Stenberg: 4-5x report rate and the reports are now genuinely good — but all LOW/MEDIUM severity, so the threat is triage-volume burnout, not a catastrophic-bug spike.

revisions.change: added the severity nuance (all curl vulns LOW/MEDIUM — triage-volume problem, not a catastrophic-bug spike) and Stenberg's 'avalanche will make maintainer overload worse' warning; noted his May 11 'greatest marketing stunt' Mythos skepticism; softened cross-references to the Mythos item from 'this morning' to 'earlier this week'; updated title, summary, why_it_matters, and brief

takes.take: The source post: 4-5x rate vs 2024, 2x vs 2025, more than one report per day on average, the quality is 'way higher than ever before,' twelve confirmed vulns with half the cycle left, on pace for 30 CVEs before half-year — and his caveat that 'almost no one finds terrible vulnerabilities,' all recent curl vulns being LOW or MEDIUM severity.

Links Stenberg's post the same day and reads it as the maintainer-side mirror of the Mythos/Glasswing story — high-capability AI vulnerability discovery is the upstream of the firehose curl's security team is now drowning in.

Centers Stenberg's own framing — AI 'augments humans in two directions, the bad way and the good way' — and notes that the same advanced analyzers that flood curl with reports are also genuinely finding deep bugs no previous tool found.

The skeptic's counterweight: Stenberg earlier called Anthropic's bug-hunting Mythos 'the greatest marketing stunt ever,' a reminder that the maintainer at the receiving end of the firehose is not sold on the framing the labs put on it.

Mirrors and discusses the post in a technical audience and adds the historical context: this is the first time in 25 years of curl's existence that the project's maintainer-burnout risk is a more pressing risk than the vulnerability backlog itself.

items.title: xAI launches Custom Skills for Grok — and Grok now reads Claude Code skills, plugins, MCPs and CLAUDE.md files with zero config
items.summary: xAI shipped Custom Skills for Grok on May 26, the third in a tight three-week run of platform primitives: Connectors (initial wave May 6 with GitHub, Notion, Linear, Google Workspace, Microsoft 365 + bring-your-own-MCP, expanded May 22 with Vercel, Canva, Gamma, and S&P Global), Skills (May 18, persistent expertise as reusable .zip / .skill / .md packs), and now Custom Skills (May 26, end-user-authored personalization that Grok remembers across every subsequent conversation). The compatibility story is sharper than the launch-day writeups first suggested: xAI's own documentation states 'Grok is fully compatible with Claude Code with zero configuration needed' — Grok automatically reads Claude Code marketplaces, plugins, skills, MCPs, agents, hooks, and instruction files (CLAUDE.md, Claude.md, CLAUDE.local.md, and .claude/rules/) alongside Grok's own .grok/ directory. In other words, xAI is shipping a feature whose file conventions are interoperable with its biggest closed-source competitor's, with no porting step. The connector list itself is the practical novelty: Grok-as-platform now natively builds and deploys websites via Vercel, generates designs in Canva, makes slide decks in Gamma, pulls live equity-market data from S&P Global, and through bring-your-own-MCP can reach any MCP server the user controls — all without leaving the Grok chat surface. Skills are live now on Grok 4.3 across grok.com, iOS, and Android; Custom Skills works on SuperGrok and X Premium subscriber tiers.
items.why_it_matters: The interesting thing in this is not Grok-becoming-a-productivity-app — that path is now well-traveled by ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and Copilot. The interesting thing is how far the compatibility move actually goes: it's not just that Grok Skills use a similar file format — xAI's docs say Grok is 'fully compatible with Claude Code with zero configuration needed' and that it reads Claude Code's marketplaces, plugins, skills, MCPs, agents, hooks, and instruction files directly. So a CLAUDE.md or .skill file you wrote for a Python project in Claude Code should be picked up by Grok with no porting at all. That kind of cross-vendor compatibility for an agent-extension primitive is rare in this market — usually the platform owners build proprietary formats specifically so switching costs stay high — and xAI's bet here is that being the friendliest place to import the rest of the industry's skills is more useful than walling off its own ecosystem. The bring-your-own-MCP support is the same pattern: Anthropic introduced MCP, xAI ships native client support without forking the protocol. For a builder, the practical read is that the agent-extension format is converging on something portable in late 2026, so time spent learning to write a good .skill or CLAUDE.md file now generalizes across more vendors than it did six months ago. For a coder broadening past Python/HTML/CSS, the deeper read is in the architectural pattern — Skills + Connectors + MCP is shaping up to be the standard three-piece kit for chat-LLM-as-a-platform (it's roughly what Cursor, Claude Code, Copilot Studio, and now Grok all converged on within about 60 days), and once the file format on the skills tier is portable across vendors, the real product question moves to what those skill packs can compose against — the connectors. Vercel/Canva/Gamma/S&P/MCP is a representative sample of what 'the LLM can drive any SaaS' actually looks like in 2026.
items.why_it_matters_brief: Grok now reads Claude Code's marketplaces, plugins, skills, MCPs, hooks and CLAUDE.md files with zero config — agent-extension formats are converging on something portable across vendors.

revisions.change: sharpened the Claude Code compatibility detail — xAI docs say Grok is 'fully compatible with Claude Code with zero configuration needed' and reads its marketplaces, plugins, skills, MCPs, agents, hooks, and instruction files directly; updated title, summary, why_it_matters, brief, and added a take

takes.take: Lays out the three-piece dev stack (Build / Skills / Connectors) and centers the format-level compatibility detail: Grok Skills are .zip / .skill / .md packs explicitly intended to drop in alongside Claude Code's skills, plugins, and CLAUDE.md files.

Quotes xAI's docs directly — 'Grok is fully compatible with Claude Code with zero configuration needed,' reading Claude Code marketplaces, plugins, skills, MCPs, agents, hooks, and instruction files (CLAUDE.md, Claude.md, CLAUDE.local.md, .claude/rules/) alongside Grok's own .grok/ directory — and dates each May feature (Grok 4.3 May 4, Build 0.1 May 14, Connectors expansion May 22, Custom Skills May 26).

Frames the May 6 / May 18 / May 22 / May 26 sequence as xAI's clearest move yet from chatbot to productivity hub, with the connector list (Vercel, Canva, Gamma, S&P Global, plus original GitHub/Notion/Linear/Google Workspace/Microsoft 365 + BYOMCP) as the practical evidence.

Picks up Musk's framing: Custom Skills lets a user teach Grok an operating method once and have Grok permanently remember the formatting rules, preferences, or workflow steps for every subsequent conversation.
Centers the bring-your-own-MCP support — Grok ships a native MCP client rather than a fork of the protocol — and reads it as xAI choosing protocol interoperability over a proprietary closed garden.

items.title: Hugging Face ships /agents.md for every Gradio Space + Kernels Hub — every Space is now machine-readable for coding agents, every GPU kernel is plug-in
items.summary: Hugging Face's May 26 release packs three builder-facing changes into one weekly push: every Gradio Space now auto-serves an /agents.md endpoint (a machine-readable API description in the same format Claude Code, Codex, OpenCode, and Pi already read from a local AGENTS.md or CLAUDE.md file), Copy to Bucket lets users move a repository directly from Hub to an S3/GCS/Azure bucket with Xet server-side transfer (large files copy in seconds), and the broader Kernels Hub is now integrated into the Transformers library and the TGI inference framework. Hugging Face's docs now treat 'Spaces as Agent Tools' as a first-class, documented pattern — point a coding agent at a Space's /agents.md and it can read the spec and call the Space's functions without any wiring. The Kernels Hub side of this is the under-the-radar systems story: 61 pre-compiled kernels (FlashAttention is the biggest, normalization layers, MoE routing, quantization), available across NVIDIA CUDA, AMD ROCm, Apple Metal, and Intel XPU, advertised at 1.7–2.5x speed-ups over baseline PyTorch and ready for torch.compile — and the original FlashAttention compile-locally pain point (96 GB RAM, several hours, brittle CUDA/PyTorch version matching) is replaced by a single line of Python that fetches a pre-compiled artifact matched to your exact hardware. The /agents.md side is the agent-discoverability story: Hugging Face Spaces went from 'demo apps a human clicks through' to 'tools an LLM can read the spec for and call directly,' which is the same architectural pattern as Microsoft's WebMCP origin trial in Chrome 149 and the broader MCP-everywhere push of the last six months. Source: huggingface.co/changelog/spaces-agents-md and huggingface.co/blog/hello-hf-kernels.
items.why_it_matters: Two builder-facing primitives ship in one release that are individually small and collectively important. The /agents.md endpoint is the small visible piece — every Gradio Space on the Hub is now a tool an LLM can introspect and call without anyone wiring it up, which is the discovery layer for agents-as-callers-of-apps that the rest of the industry is still figuring out, and Hugging Face has now made it a documented 'Spaces as Agent Tools' pattern rather than an experiment. The deeper play is the Kernels Hub. FlashAttention has been the single most painful 'I cannot compile this on my laptop' rite-of-passage for hobbyist ML work for two years (96 GB of RAM and several hours of compile time per CUDA/PyTorch combination, brittle to version mismatches), and Hugging Face has moved compilation to the cloud and exposes it as a one-line Python import that picks up your exact hardware. For a Python coder, the practical read is direct: any project that touches Transformer inference (Whisper for audio, a local LLM, image generation) just got 1.7-2.5x faster with no source code change beyond the kernel import — and that holds across NVIDIA CUDA, AMD ROCm, Apple Metal, and Intel XPU, which is the right cross-hardware portability story for a hobbyist who is not married to one GPU vendor. The pattern across this Hugging Face week is the same as the xAI Skills + Connectors story in this digest: builder-platform owners are converging on a small set of portable primitives (machine-readable agent specs, plug-in GPU kernels, MCP everywhere) that are explicitly designed not to lock developers in. That is genuinely good news for hobbyist coders, because it means time spent learning these primitives generalizes across more places than the proprietary alternatives of two years ago did.
items.why_it_matters_brief: FlashAttention without 96 GB of compile-time RAM, every Gradio Space machine-readable for agents — builder-platform primitives are converging on something portable.

revisions.change: light enrich — Hugging Face now documents 'Spaces as Agent Tools' as a first-class pattern and lists Claude Code, Codex, OpenCode, and Pi as agents that can consume the /agents.md endpoint; no major new developments in the 24h window

takes.take: Auto-serves /agents.md on every Gradio Space; point Claude Code, Codex, OpenCode, or Pi at it and the agent can read the spec and call the Space's functions without any wiring. Same machine-readable convention as a local AGENTS.md or CLAUDE.md file.

61 pre-compiled kernels (FlashAttention, normalization, MoE routing, quantization), 1.7-2.5x over baseline PyTorch, torch.compile-ready, supports NVIDIA CUDA, AMD ROCm, Apple Metal, and Intel XPU — one Python import replaces a 96GB / several-hour local compile.

Pitches the simplification directly: 'what if shipping a GPU kernel was as easy as pushing a model?' — pre-compiled for your exact GPU, PyTorch, and OS; multiple kernel versions coexist in one process; torch.compile compatible.

Picks up the cross-vendor framing — same kernels available across the four major hardware vendors — and notes integration into the Transformers library and TGI as the practical paths developers will actually hit it.

items.title: Gemini API's Interactions schema flips to the new shape on May 26 — legacy schema removed on June 8
items.summary: Google's May 26 default-switch for the Gemini v1beta Interactions API is the kind of mechanical-but-load-bearing migration that gets missed in I/O recaps. The change replaces the legacy `outputs` array with a structured `steps` array (a polymorphic timeline of `user_input`, `model_output`, `function_call`, `thought`, `google_search_call`, `google_search_result`, and tool-specific step types) and consolidates output format controls into a new polymorphic `response_format` (which removes the old `response_mime_type` field). Google says the new shape is what's needed for future capabilities like mid-flight steering and asynchronous tool calls — but the immediately load-bearing detail is the timeline: opt-in via `Api-Revision: 2026-05-20` was available from May 20, the new schema became the default for all requests on May 26, explicit opt-out via `Api-Revision: 2026-05-07` survives until June 8, and on June 8 the legacy schema is removed entirely. Two operational details surfaced in the migration guidance. First, the streaming path changes too — clients must listen for new SSE event types (`interaction.created`, `step.delta`) and conversation history is now managed by passing the `steps` array back in the next request's `input` field, not the old flat output list. Second, Google ships an automated migration skill in the Gemini Skills Library: running `/gemini-interactions-api migrate my app` performs the mechanical code changes for you. Updated Python and JavaScript SDKs (≥2.0.0) automatically opt callers into the new schema and only support the new shape. Any production Gemini API code that doesn't migrate by June 8 will break. Source: ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/interactions-breaking-changes-may-2026.
items.why_it_matters: This is in the digest specifically because it's the kind of breaking change a hobbyist Python project hits without warning. If a script or side project is using the Gemini Python SDK and reads `response.outputs[0]`, that code path now returns either nothing or an unexpected shape (depending on which header was sent), and stops working entirely on June 8. The substantive reason for the change is the architectural one: the new `steps` timeline supports mid-flight steering (interrupting an in-progress generation) and asynchronous tool calls (the model fires a tool call and continues working on other steps while the tool runs) — both of which require a polymorphic timeline structure that the old flat `outputs` array couldn't express. That's the same architectural shape as Claude's tool-use and OpenAI's Responses API steps, so the three big LLM APIs are now converging on roughly the same response model. The good news on cost: the migration is genuinely small (upgrade google-genai to ≥2.0.0, change `for o in response.outputs` to `for s in response.steps` and dispatch on step type), and Google now ships an automated migration skill — `/gemini-interactions-api migrate my app` in the Gemini Skills Library does the mechanical rewrite — but it really does need to happen before June 8. If your code streams responses, note the streaming path changed too: you now listen for `interaction.created` / `step.delta` SSE events and feed the `steps` array back as `input` for history. The deeper takeaway is the convergence: if you're learning one tool-use response model right now, learn the polymorphic-timeline shape, because that's what all three of OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google are now standardizing on, and what every agent framework (Claude Code, Cursor, LangChain, LlamaIndex, Mistral Vibe) ultimately speaks against.
items.why_it_matters_brief: If your code reads `response.outputs`, it breaks June 8 — the new `steps` shape matches OpenAI/Anthropic, and Google now ships an automated migration skill to do the mechanical rewrite.

revisions.change: added the streaming-path changes (new SSE event types `interaction.created` / `step.delta`, history managed via the `steps` array in `input`), the new `thought` step type, and Google's automated migration skill (`/gemini-interactions-api migrate my app`); updated summary, why_it_matters, brief, and added a take

takes.take: The canonical migration doc: new `steps` array, new polymorphic `response_format`, opt-in May 20, default May 26, last day for explicit opt-out June 8, legacy schema removed entirely after that. Streaming clients must handle new SSE event types and pass `steps` back as `input` for history.

Documents the automated migration skill — running `/gemini-interactions-api migrate my app` handles the mechanical code changes (outputs → steps, response_mime_type → response_format, history reshaping) so the migration cost is mostly review rather than hand-editing.

Reads it as the most urgent quiet breaking change of the May 2026 cycle — emphasizes the silent-failure mode where SDK ≥2.0.0 callers don't see the migration deadline because the SDK already auto-opted them in.

Anchors the original announcement and the rationale: the new schema is the prerequisite for mid-flight steering and async tool calls, both of which Google has signaled as upcoming Gemini API capabilities through Q3 2026.

items.title: Mistral's AI Now Summit in Paris launches Workflows — Temporal-powered Python orchestration handling millions of daily executions, plus a new French data center
items.summary: Mistral AI held its AI Now Summit in Paris on May 28, showcasing Workflows — its Python-first orchestration layer for enterprise AI — alongside a new 10MW inference facility in Les Ulis, France (south of Paris), scheduled to open Q3 2026. Workflows, first entered public preview in late April 2026, is built on Temporal's durable-execution engine (the same infrastructure powering orchestration at Netflix, Stripe, and Salesforce) and extended for AI-specific workloads with streaming, payload handling, multi-tenancy, and observability. By the time of the summit, Mistral reports the system is already handling millions of daily executions. The Python SDK (pip install mistralai-workflows, v3.0) lets developers write workflow logic in plain Python; workers deploy on the developer's own Kubernetes cluster via Helm chart, while the Mistral-controlled control plane handles orchestration — a data-residency model aimed at regulated industries where data cannot leave specific geographies. The three-layer Mistral Studio stack is now complete: Forge (custom model training, March 2026) at the base, Workflows (orchestration) in the middle, Vibe (coding agent and long-running task execution) at the top. In a separate CNBC interview published May 28, CEO Arthur Mensch said Mistral is exploring designing its own chips — noting it is 'of course, interesting' and the company is 'not ruling it out,' following the path Amazon (Trainium), Google (TPU), and Microsoft (Maia) have taken. Mensch also gave a direct response to Pope Leo XIV's Vatican encyclical 'Magnifica Humanitas' (covered in 2026-05-25-am), saying he rejects criticism of AI military use and argues Europe needs its own AI tools to deter adversaries — a more pointed pushback than any US frontier lab has offered.
items.why_it_matters: Workflows is the most practically significant thing Mistral has shipped since Mistral Medium 3.5 (April 29). The Temporal foundation is the load-bearing technical detail: Temporal provides durable execution — multi-step processes that survive failures, retries, and timeouts without restarting from scratch — battle-tested at Netflix, Stripe, Salesforce, and Uber, and now adapted specifically for LLM pipelines. What that means in practice: a Mistral Workflow can coordinate a chain of model calls, human-in-the-loop approvals, external API calls, and conditional logic, and if any step fails the engine retries from the last successful checkpoint rather than re-running from zero. That reliability guarantee is what moves orchestration from a research demo to a production runtime, and it's the same shape LangChain, LlamaIndex, and AWS Step Functions-for-AI are all competing to provide. For a Python coder, the direct entry point is pip install mistralai-workflows — workflow steps are plain Python, not a custom DSL. The data-residency model (workers in your cluster, control plane at Mistral) is aimed at EU-regulated businesses (healthcare, finance, GDPR-intensive) where data can't leave specific geographies. The completeness of the three-layer stack (Forge → Workflows → Vibe) now positions Mistral as the only European frontier lab with a full training-to-orchestration-to-agentic-execution platform under one vendor — a meaningful differentiator for European enterprises weighing US hyperscaler dependency. The chip-design ambition is speculative but directional: if Mistral follows Amazon/Google, it signals long-term intent to internalize silicon costs. Mensch's direct rebuke of the Vatican encyclical is the editorial bookend: where US frontier-lab CEOs have responded with diplomatic noises, he's taking an explicit position on a contested policy question — which is either the most European thing possible or an early signal that the EU AI Act's governance norms are being tested by the firms it regulates.
items.why_it_matters_brief: Mistral's Workflows puts a Temporal-powered durable-execution engine behind Python AI chains — same reliability model as Netflix/Stripe orchestration, runs in your own Kubernetes cluster, pip-installable.

revisions.change: created; AI Now Summit Paris today (May 28) prompted VentureBeat deep-dive and CNBC CEO interview; gap item — Workflows first previewed late April, no prior snapshot coverage

takes.take: Deepest technical coverage: Temporal foundation ('same infrastructure that powers orchestration at Netflix, Stripe, and Salesforce'), the 'already running millions of daily executions' scale signal, and the AI-specific adaptations (streaming, payload handling, multi-tenancy) Mistral added on top.

Canonical product page: three-part stack diagram (Forge → Workflows → Vibe), Python SDK installation instructions, RBAC access controls and full audit trail, Kubernetes-based data-residency deployment model.

CEO Mensch: Mistral is 'not ruling out' designing its own chips, called it 'of course interesting,' framing it against Amazon/Google hyperscaler precedent; also announced the 10MW Les Ulis inference facility as part of €4B data-center buildout.

Enterprise/developer context from the April preview: centers the Temporal architecture choice, Kubernetes worker deployment, and why durable execution matters for LLM pipelines where probabilistic model output sits on top of deterministic business rules.
Frames Workflows as Mistral's move from model API to full enterprise AI stack — the competitive context versus LangChain, AWS Step Functions for Bedrock, and Temporal.io natively.

items.title: NVIDIA's GTC Taipei keynote is June 1 — Huang calls Vera Rubin 'the largest product launch in Taiwan's history,' with AMD Venice on TSMC 2nm in the same week
items.summary: Jensen Huang will deliver the NVIDIA GTC Taipei keynote at the Taipei Music Center at 11 a.m. Taiwan Time on Monday June 1 (Sunday May 31 8 p.m. PT), opening NVIDIA's three-day GTC Taipei conference and running into COMPUTEX 2026 (June 2-5, 1,500 exhibitors, 6,000 booths); a free pregame livestream with industry leaders runs at 9 a.m. Taiwan Time, two hours before the keynote. NVIDIA has previewed the keynote as the showcase of its 'Five-Layer Cake' approach (from energy to applications) and the Vera Rubin ramp — and Huang has now framed the stakes himself, telling press that 'Vera Rubin is the largest product launch, probably in the history of Taiwan.' The showcase piece is reported to be the Vera Rubin NVL72 rack-scale architecture (a next-generation rack designed to house the new Vera CPUs, rumored to outperform standard x86 enterprise silicon by ~1.5x), with coverage suggesting the keynote leans toward enterprise-AI-infrastructure and physical AI rather than the consumer N1 platform earlier rumors centered on. In pre-keynote press interactions, Huang added a notable wildcard: 'The second half of this year is going to be very, very busy with Grace Blackwell, Vera Rubin, and we have a surprise new product that we haven't told anyone about yet.' Two non-NVIDIA stories are now baked into the same week's framing. First, AMD Venice (EPYC 6th Gen, 256 cores, ~70% per-core perf uplift on the same socket) is in volume production at TSMC on the 2nm process — the first HPC product to ship in volume on TSMC's N2 node, with the follow-on Verano part already taped out. Second, in his pre-keynote remarks in Taiwan (CNA, TechTimes, AI1G) Huang told the press that splitting the world into separate US and China AI ecosystems would be 'not wise,' acknowledged that NVIDIA has 'largely conceded' the China AI-chip market to Huawei after US export controls left a vacuum, and confirmed NVIDIA's most recent earnings reported zero China revenue. Bloomberg and TechTimes report Huang flew to TSMC the same week as Vera Rubin ramp constraints stress the Taiwan supply chain. The keynote itself is now three days out — this item is the pre-event scene-setter; substantive product news will land June 1.
items.why_it_matters: The NVIDIA GTC Taipei keynote is the single biggest planned AI hardware event left in Q2 2026, and the framing around it has shifted in the last two weeks in ways worth tracking before Monday. Huang's own 'largest product launch, probably in the history of Taiwan' line tells you NVIDIA is treating Vera Rubin — and specifically the NVL72 rack-scale architecture — as the centerpiece, not the rumored consumer N1 part, so calibrate for a data-center-and-physical-AI keynote rather than a consumer one. The new pre-keynote wildcard is Huang's hint at a 'surprise new product that we haven't told anyone about yet' alongside Vera Rubin and Grace Blackwell in H2 2026 — worth tracking to see what surfaces Monday. First, AMD Venice in volume production at TSMC 2nm is the cleanest signal yet that the AMD-as-real-alternative narrative has moved from 'maybe' to 'measurable' — 256 cores, ~70% per-core uplift on the same socket, and the first HPC product on TSMC N2 in volume, paired with the Zyphra ZAYA1-8B training-from-scratch-on-AMD result from earlier in May, mean an HTML/CSS/Python coder reading the keynote next Monday should be calibrated for a more multi-vendor story than the NVIDIA-only story of the last three years. Second, Huang's own 'we have largely conceded China to Huawei' and 'zero China revenue' framing is structurally important and was barely covered in the I/O recaps: it tells you that the US-export-controls story has now produced its first durable second-order effect (Huawei is now NVIDIA's effective monopolist in the Chinese-market segment), which is going to land in every chip-and-AI-export discussion through the rest of 2026. For your stack, neither of these is a near-term tooling decision — you write Python, you don't pick a data-center chip — but both are the right kind of horizon-level signals to absorb. The Five-Layer Cake framing NVIDIA has trailed for the keynote (energy → silicon → networking → software → applications) is the architecture-of-the-stack story that the rest of the AI industry has been quietly converging on, and the actual model and platform announcements on Monday will be measured against it.
items.why_it_matters_brief: NVIDIA's GTC Taipei keynote lands Monday — Huang calls Vera Rubin 'the largest product launch in Taiwan's history' and hints at a 'surprise new product' in H2, while AMD Venice in volume on TSMC 2nm makes it a more multi-vendor frame.

revisions.change: added Huang's 'largest product launch probably in the history of Taiwan' framing, the Vera Rubin NVL72 rack-scale architecture detail (~1.5x over x86), the 9 a.m. pregame livestream, and the reporting that the keynote leans enterprise/physical-AI rather than the consumer N1; updated title, summary, why_it_matters, brief, and added a take
added Huang's pre-keynote 'surprise new product that we haven't told anyone about yet' quote from WccfTech; updated keynote countdown (now 3 days out); updated why_it_matters_brief

takes.take: Official keynote scene-setter — Taipei Music Center, June 1 at 11 a.m. Taiwan / May 31 8 p.m. PT, free 9 a.m. pregame livestream, 'Five-Layer Cake' framing from energy to applications, with the Vera Rubin ramp as the planned showcase piece.

Confirms the June 1 keynote date (the day before Computex opens) and treats it as the moment the Vera Rubin ramp story has to either reassure or worry the supply-chain-watching audience after weeks of constraint reports.

Carries Huang's 'Vera Rubin is the largest product launch, probably in the history of Taiwan' line and his new pre-keynote hint: 'The second half of this year is going to be very, very busy with Grace Blackwell, Vera Rubin, and we have a surprise new product that we haven't told anyone about yet.'

Reports Huang's mid-week TSMC visit and frames the Vera Rubin ramp as actively straining the Taiwan AI supply chain, which is the load-bearing context for whatever supply-chain narrative the keynote chooses to tell.

Quotes Huang's pre-keynote remarks in Taiwan — 'compute equals revenues,' 'splitting the world into separate US and China AI ecosystems is not wise,' confirmation NVIDIA has 'largely conceded' the China AI-chip market to Huawei — the most newsworthy framing of the week.
